title: Client Report - Famous Names
text:
QUESTION 1

Make a new column that represents the total number of babies with your name for only those states in which you have lived. (If you have only ever lived in one state, pick at least one other state so you get practice summing across columns). Make a graph displaying that total for your name over time.
First I need to filter my name out of the file, then select my name from the states I’ve lived which is AZ and ID


Show the code
# Q1
ash = df.filter(pl.col("name") == "Ash")
states = ash.with_columns(
  (pl.col("AZ") + pl.col("ID")).alias("total")
)

(
  ggplot(states, aes(x="year", y="total"))
  +geom_line(color="blue", size=1)
  +scale_x_continuous(format="d")
  +geom_vline(xintercept=2024, color="red", linetype=2)
  +geom_text(x=2023, y= -1, color='red', hjust=1, label="When I first came to Idaho (2024)")
)

title: Client Report - Famous Names
text:
QUESTION 2

Let’s look at a name that is special for members of the church: Brigham. For each year, calculate what proportion of babies named Brigham were born in Idaho and Utah? Make a graph displaying the proportion over time.

First we filter out the name brigam and calculate it’s percent, and then we plot out the names popularity throughout the years. We can see a gaint spike down in the year 1992


Show the code
# Q2

brig = df.filter(pl.col("name") == "Brigham")
states_percent = brig.with_columns(
  ((pl.col("UT") + pl.col("ID"))/pl.col("Total")).alias("percent")
)

(
  ggplot(states_percent, aes(x="year", y="percent"))
  +scale_x_continuous(format="d")
  +geom_line(color="blue", size =1)
  +labs(
    title="Name in Brigham born in Idaho and Utah percent",
    x="Year",
    y="Percent"
  )
)

title: Client Report - Famous Names
text:
QUESTION 3

Consider doing some data validation. For the name David, calculate a sum of all 50 states and District of Columbia for each year. Now compare that total to the value in the ‘Total’ column. In which years, if any, do they not agree?

First we filter out the name David, then we total the states. which would be to add all the columns excluding the name, year, and total columns. saving this total as a new column called state_sum. then we find where our calculation doesn’t match the total calculation


Show the code
# Q3
david = df.filter(pl.col("name") == "David")

sum = david.with_columns(
    pl.sum_horizontal(pl.exclude("name", "year", "Total")).alias("state_sum")
)

not_match = sum.filter(pl.col("state_sum") != pl.col("Total"))

if not_match.height > 0:
  print("the years where they don'y agree:")
  print(not_match.select("year", "state_sum", "Total"))
else:
  print("They all match!")


They all match!

QUESTION 1

Create a new ‘season’ column. Define ‘season’ as:

Winter for January, Febuary (note the mispelling), March
Spring for April, May, June
Summer for July, August, September
Fall for October, November, December
All other rows can be assigned a value of ‘Unknown’


No discussion needed for this question


Show the code
# Include and execute your code here
seasons = df.with_columns(
  seasons = pl.when(pl.col('month').is_in(['January','Febuary','March']))
    .then(pl.lit('Winter'))
    .when(pl.col('month').is_in(['April','May','June']))
    .then(pl.lit('Spring'))
    .when(pl.col('month').is_in(['July','August','September']))
    .then(pl.lit('Summer'))
    .when(pl.col('month').is_in(['October','November','December']))
    .then(pl.lit('Fall'))
    .otherwise(pl.lit('Unknown'))
)

title: Client Report - Flights: Creating New Columns
text:
QUESTION 2

Divide all the columns by 1000 whose name starts with num_of. Be sure to first filter out rows that contain problematic values for the columns like -999 or 1500+.

No discussion needed for this question


Show the code
# Include and execute your code here
df_clean = (
    seasons.with_columns(
        pl.col("^num_of.*$")
        .cast(pl.String)               # ensure string can use string or utf8
        .str.replace(r"\+", "")        # remove + 
        .cast(pl.Float64)              # back to number
    )
    .filter(
        pl.all_horizontal(
            (pl.col("^num_of.*$") != 999) &
            (pl.col("^num_of.*$") != 1500)
        )
    )
    .with_columns(
        (pl.col("^num_of.*$") / 1000)
    )
)

title: Client Report - Flights: Creating New Columns
text:
QUESTION 3

According to the BTS website, the “Weather” category only accounts for severe weather delays. Mild weather delays are not counted in the “Weather” category, but are actually included in both the “nas” and “late_aircraft” categories. Using the columns you modified in the bullet above, your job is to create a new column that calculates the total number of flights (in 1,000’s) delayed by weather (both severe and mild). Show your work by printing the first 5 rows of the dataset. Use these three rules for your calculations:

100% of delayed flights in the Weather category are due to weather
num_of_delays_weather
30% of all delayed flights in the “late_aircraft” category are due to weather
num_of_delays_late_aircraft
From April through August, 40% of delayed flights in the NAS category are due to weather. The rest of the months, the proportion rises to 65%
num_of_delays_nas April–August → 40% All other months → 65%


No discussion needed for this question


Show the code
# Include and execute your code here
# weather_total =
#     num_of_delays_weather
#   + 0.30 * num_of_delays_late_aircraft
#   + NAS_factor * num_of_delays_nas

delays = df_clean.with_columns(
  Delay_Calculation = pl.col('num_of_delays_weather') 
    + (pl.col('num_of_delays_late_aircraft') * 0.30) 
    +( pl.when(pl.col('month').is_in(['April', 'May', 'June', 'July', 'August']))
      .then(0.40 * pl.col('num_of_delays_nas'))
      .otherwise(0.65 * pl.col('num_of_delays_nas'))
    )
)

from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier

KNeighborsClassifier()In a Jupyter environment, please rerun this cell to show the HTML representation or trust the notebook. On GitHub, the HTML representation is unable to render, please try loading this page with nbviewer.org.KNeighborsClassifier?Documentation for KNeighborsClassifieriFitted
        
            
                Parameters
                









n_neighbors n_neighbors: int, default=5

Number of neighbors to use by default for :meth:`kneighbors` queries.
5



weights weights: {'uniform', 'distance'}, callable or None, default='uniform'

Weight function used in prediction. Possible values:

- 'uniform' : uniform weights. All points in each neighborhood
are weighted equally.
- 'distance' : weight points by the inverse of their distance.
in this case, closer neighbors of a query point will have a
greater influence than neighbors which are further away.
- [callable] : a user-defined function which accepts an
array of distances, and returns an array of the same shape
containing the weights.

Refer to the example entitled
:ref:`sphx_glr_auto_examples_neighbors_plot_classification.py`
showing the impact of the `weights` parameter on the decision
boundary.
'uniform'



algorithm algorithm: {'auto', 'ball_tree', 'kd_tree', 'brute'}, default='auto'

Algorithm used to compute the nearest neighbors:

- 'ball_tree' will use :class:`BallTree`
- 'kd_tree' will use :class:`KDTree`
- 'brute' will use a brute-force search.
- 'auto' will attempt to decide the most appropriate algorithm
based on the values passed to :meth:`fit` method.

Note: fitting on sparse input will override the setting of
this parameter, using brute force.
'auto'



leaf_size leaf_size: int, default=30

Leaf size passed to BallTree or KDTree. This can affect the
speed of the construction and query, as well as the memory
required to store the tree. The optimal value depends on the
nature of the problem.
30



p p: float, default=2

Power parameter for the Minkowski metric. When p = 1, this is equivalent
to using manhattan_distance (l1), and euclidean_distance (l2) for p = 2.
For arbitrary p, minkowski_distance (l_p) is used. This parameter is expected
to be positive.
2



metric metric: str or callable, default='minkowski'

Metric to use for distance computation. Default is "minkowski", which
results in the standard Euclidean distance when p = 2. See the
documentation of `scipy.spatial.distance
< docs.scipy.org doc scipy reference spatial.distance.html>`_ and
the metrics listed in
:class:`~sklearn.metrics.pairwise.distance_metrics` for valid metric
values.

If metric is "precomputed", X is assumed to be a distance matrix and
must be square during fit. X may be a :term:`sparse graph`, in which
case only "nonzero" elements may be considered neighbors.

If metric is a callable function, it takes two arrays representing 1D
vectors as inputs and must return one value indicating the distance
between those vectors. This works for Scipy's metrics, but is less
efficient than passing the metric name as a string.
'minkowski'



metric_params metric_params: dict, default=None

Additional keyword arguments for the metric function.
None



n_jobs n_jobs: int, default=None

The number of parallel jobs to run for neighbors search.
``None`` means 1 unless in a :obj:`joblib.parallel_backend` context.
``-1`` means using all processors. See :term:`Glossary `
for more details.
Doesn't affect :meth:`fit` method.
None

q = """
  SELECT 
    playerID, 
    yearID, 
    ROUND((1.0 * SUM(H) / SUM(AB)), 3 ) AS 'Batting_avg' 
  FROM batting
  GROUP BY playerID
  HAVING SUM(AB) >= 100
  ORDER BY Batting_avg desc, playerID asc
  LIMIT 5

title: Client Report - Joins
text:
QUESTION 2
1. Pick any two baseball teams and compare them using a metric of your choice (average salary, home runs, number of wins, etc). Be creative! Write an SQL query to read in the table(s) you need, then use polars to manipulate the data. Finally, make a graph using Lets-Plot to visualize the comparison. What do you learn?
I decided to compare the Philies to the Cardinals using the metric salary. After taking a step back and looking at the comparison one would feel that there really isn’t much of a difference. But in reality the difference between their averages is $110,000! If I had to chose a team to play on I would go with the Phillies because that is quite a bonus in comparison to the Cardinals


Show the code
# Include and execute your code here
q='''
SELECT teamID, AVG(salary) AS 'Avg_salary' FROM salaries WHERE teamID IN('PHI', 'SLN') GROUP BY teamID
'''
salaries = pl.read_database(q, conn)

salaries=salaries.with_columns(
  pl.when(pl.col('teamID') == 'PHI').then(pl.lit("Phillies"))
  .otherwise(pl.lit('Cardinals')).alias("renamedTeamID")
)

(
  ggplot(salaries,aes(x='renamedTeamID', y='Avg_salary'))
  +geom_bar(stat='identity')
  +labs(
    title='Comparision of Salaries',
    x='The teams',
    y='Salary average'
  )
)

Describe the quality of your classification model using 2-3 different evaluation metrics. You also need to explain how to define/interpret each of the evaluation metrics you use.

The different evaluation metrics that I used were classification report and confusion matrix. USing these we can then see the percentage of how acurate our predictions were. from what we did we got high 80’s when it came to our predictions which is a good percentage for our predictions

x = df.select((cs.numeric()).exclude(["before1980","yrbuilt"]))

from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

Show the code
from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeClassifier
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.neighbors import KNeighborsClassifier

# Use .to_series() to flatten the target column without manual numpy calls
y_train_series = y_train.to_series()

tree_classifier = DecisionTreeClassifier()
tree_classifier.fit(x_train, y_train_series)

forest_classifier = RandomForestClassifier()
forest_classifier.fit(x_train, y_train_series)

knee_classifier = KNeighborsClassifier()
knee_classifier.fit(x_train, y_train_series)


KNeighborsClassifier()In a Jupyter environment, please rerun this cell to show the HTML representation or trust the notebook. On GitHub, the HTML representation is unable to render, please try loading this page with nbviewer.org.KNeighborsClassifier?Documentation for KNeighborsClassifieriFitted
        
            
                Parameters
                









n_neighbors n_neighbors: int, default=5

Number of neighbors to use by default for :meth:`kneighbors` queries.
5



weights weights: {'uniform', 'distance'}, callable or None, default='uniform'

Weight function used in prediction. Possible values:

- 'uniform' : uniform weights. All points in each neighborhood
are weighted equally.
- 'distance' : weight points by the inverse of their distance.
in this case, closer neighbors of a query point will have a
greater influence than neighbors which are further away.
- [callable] : a user-defined function which accepts an
array of distances, and returns an array of the same shape
containing the weights.

Refer to the example entitled
:ref:`sphx_glr_auto_examples_neighbors_plot_classification.py`
showing the impact of the `weights` parameter on the decision
boundary.
'uniform'



algorithm algorithm: {'auto', 'ball_tree', 'kd_tree', 'brute'}, default='auto'

Algorithm used to compute the nearest neighbors:

- 'ball_tree' will use :class:`BallTree`
- 'kd_tree' will use :class:`KDTree`
- 'brute' will use a brute-force search.
- 'auto' will attempt to decide the most appropriate algorithm
based on the values passed to :meth:`fit` method.

Note: fitting on sparse input will override the setting of
this parameter, using brute force.
'auto'



leaf_size leaf_size: int, default=30

Leaf size passed to BallTree or KDTree. This can affect the
speed of the construction and query, as well as the memory
required to store the tree. The optimal value depends on the
nature of the problem.
30



p p: float, default=2

Power parameter for the Minkowski metric. When p = 1, this is equivalent
to using manhattan_distance (l1), and euclidean_distance (l2) for p = 2.
For arbitrary p, minkowski_distance (l_p) is used. This parameter is expected
to be positive.
2



metric metric: str or callable, default='minkowski'

Metric to use for distance computation. Default is "minkowski", which
results in the standard Euclidean distance when p = 2. See the
documentation of `scipy.spatial.distance
< docs.scipy.org doc scipy reference spatial.distance.html>`_ and
the metrics listed in
:class:`~sklearn.metrics.pairwise.distance_metrics` for valid metric
values.

If metric is "precomputed", X is assumed to be a distance matrix and
must be square during fit. X may be a :term:`sparse graph`, in which
case only "nonzero" elements may be considered neighbors.

If metric is a callable function, it takes two arrays representing 1D
vectors as inputs and must return one value indicating the distance
between those vectors. This works for Scipy's metrics, but is less
efficient than passing the metric name as a string.
'minkowski'



metric_params metric_params: dict, default=None

Additional keyword arguments for the metric function.
None



n_jobs n_jobs: int, default=None

The number of parallel jobs to run for neighbors search.
``None`` means 1 unless in a :obj:`joblib.parallel_backend` context.
``-1`` means using all processors. See :term:`Glossary `
for more details.
Doesn't affect :meth:`fit` method.
None

The maximum depth of the tree. If None, then nodes are expanded until
all leaves are pure or until all leaves contain less than

The minimum number of samples required to be at a leaf node.

least ``min_samples_leaf`` training samples in each of the left and
right branches. This may have the effect of smoothing the model,
especially in regression.

- If int, then consider `min_samples_leaf` as the minimum number.
- If float, then `min_samples_leaf` is a fraction and
`ceil(min_samples_leaf * n_samples)` are the minimum
number of samples for each node.

Added float values for fractions.

equal weight when sample_weight is not provided.
0.0



max_features max_features: int, float or {"sqrt", "log2"}, default=None

The number of features to consider when looking for the best split:

- If int, then consider `max_features` features at each split.
- If float, then `max_features` is a fraction and
`max(1, int(max_features * n_features_in_))` features are considered at
each split.
- If "sqrt", then `max_features=sqrt(n_features)`.
- If "log2", then `max_features=log2(n_features)`.
- If None, then `max_features=n_features`.

.. note::

The search for a split does not stop until at least one
valid partition of the node samples is found, even if it requires to
effectively inspect more than ``max_features`` features.
None



random_state random_state: int, RandomState instance or None, default=None

Controls the randomness of the estimator. The features are always
randomly permuted at each split, even if ``splitter`` is set to
``"best"``. When ``max_features < n_features``, the algorithm will
select ``max_features`` at random at each split before finding the best
split among them. But the best found split may vary across different
runs, even if ``max_features=n_features``. That is the case, if the
improvement of the criterion is identical for several splits and one
split has to be selected at random. To obtain a deterministic behaviour
during fitting, ``random_state`` has to be fixed to an integer.
See :term:`Glossary ` for details.
None



max_leaf_nodes max_leaf_nodes: int, default=None

Best nodes are defined as relative reduction in impurity.
If None then unlimited number of leaf nodes.
None



min_impurity_decrease min_impurity_decrease: float, default=0.0

A node will be split if this split induces a decrease of the impurity
greater than or equal to this value.

The weighted impurity decrease equation is the following::

N_t / N * (impurity - N_t_R / N_t * right_impurity
- N_t_L / N_t * left_impurity)

where ``N`` is the total number of samples, ``N_t`` is the number of
samples at the current node, ``N_t_L`` is the number of samples in the
left child, and ``N_t_R`` is the number of samples in the right child.

``N``, ``N_t``, ``N_t_R`` and ``N_t_L`` all refer to the weighted sum,
if ``sample_weight`` is passed.

.. versionadded:: 0.19
0.0



class_weight class_weight: dict, list of dict or "balanced", default=None

Weights associated with classes in the form ``{class_label: weight}``.
If None, all classes are supposed to have weight one. For
multi-output problems, a list of dicts can be provided in the same
order as the columns of y.

Note that for multioutput (including multilabel) weights should be
defined for each class of every column in its own dict. For example,
for four-class multilabel classification weights should be
[{0: 1, 1: 1}, {0: 1, 1: 5}, {0: 1, 1: 1}, {0: 1, 1: 1}] instead of
[{1:1}, {2:5}, {3:1}, {4:1}].

The "balanced" mode uses the values of y to automatically adjust
weights inversely proportional to class frequencies in the input data
as ``n_samples / (n_classes * np.bincount(y))``

For multi-output, the weights of each column of y will be multiplied.

Note that these weights will be multiplied with sample_weight (passed
through the fit method) if sample_weight is specified.
None

Indicates the monotonicity constraint to enforce on each feature.

Indicates the monotonicity constraint to enforce on each feature.
- 1: monotonically increasing
- 0: no constraint
- -1: monotonically decreasing

If monotonic_cst is None, no constraints are applied.

Monotonicity constraints are not supported for:
- multioutput regressions (i.e. when `n_outputs_ > 1`),
- regressions trained on data with missing values.

QUESTION 1

Which airport has the worst delays? Describe the metric you chose, and why you chose it to determine the “worst” airport. Your answer should include a summary table that lists (for each airport) the total number of flights, total number of delayed flights, proportion of delayed flights, and average delay time in hours.

By using the group by for the airport name we can then do the calculations for all 4 columns givin in the question.


Show the code
# Include and execute your code here
delays = (
  df.group_by('airport_name')
  .agg(
    pl.col('num_of_flights_total').sum().alias('Total_flights'),  #total number of flights
    pl.col('num_of_delays_total').sum().alias('Total_delays'),   #total number of delayed flights
    (pl.col('num_of_delays_total').sum() / pl.col('num_of_flights_total').sum()).alias('proportion_delayed'),    #proportion of delayed flights
    ((pl.col('minutes_delayed_total').sum() / pl.col('num_of_flights_total').sum()) / 60).alias('avg_delay_hours'),   #average delay time in hours.
  )
)

1. Which delay is the worst delay? Keep working with the cleaned dataset you created in the previous task. Compare the proportion of flights delayed due to Carrier, to Security, and to Late Aircraft. Show this comparison in a Chart and a Table. Describe your results.
After creating a bar chart comapring the sums of each cause for delays and comparing them to eachother it is very easy to see that the main cause for delays is late aircrafts. As we can see late aircraft is responsible for 62% of the delays, whereas carrier is responsible for 37%, and security is the 0.32% of delays


Show the code
total_delays = cleaned.select([
    pl.col("num_of_delays_carrier").cast(pl.Int64).sum().alias("carrier"),
    pl.col("num_of_delays_security").cast(pl.Int64).sum().alias("security"),
    pl.col("num_of_delays_late_aircraft").cast(pl.Int64).sum().alias("late_aircraft")
])

# Convert wide to long
delays_long = total_delays.unpivot(
    index=[],  # no identifier needed, just sum everything
    on=["carrier", "late_aircraft", "security"],
    variable_name="delay_type",
    value_name="count"
).with_columns(
  proportion = pl.col("count") / pl.col("count").sum()
)

# Step 1: sum delays per airport
delays_per_airport = (
    cleaned.group_by("airport_code")  
    .agg([
      pl.col("num_of_delays_carrier").cast(pl.Int64).sum().alias("carrier"),
      pl.col("num_of_delays_late_aircraft").cast(pl.Int64).sum().alias("late_aircraft"),
      pl.col("num_of_delays_security").cast(pl.Int64).sum().alias("security")
    ])
)

# Step 2: convert wide -> long using unpivot (same style as total_delays)
delays_long_per_airport = delays_per_airport.unpivot(
    index=["airport_code"],  # keep airport_code as identifier
    on=["carrier", "late_aircraft","security"],
    variable_name="delay_type",
    value_name="Count"
).with_columns(
  proportion = pl.col("Count") / pl.col("Count").sum().over("airport_code")
)

title: Client Report - What’s in a Name?
text:
QUESTION 1

How does your name at your birth year compare to its use historically? Your must provide a chart. The years labels on your charts should not include a comma.
After filtering out all the names besides my own, I then created a graph to compare it’s popluarity over time. As seen in the graph the red line represents the year I was born which is 2006. Before the year 2005 ‘Ash’ was a very unique name that very few people had, but after I was born it’s popularity has been continously growing as the years go by.


Show the code
# Q1
# 1. Filter for the name
ash = df.filter(pl.col("name") == "Ash")
birth_year = 2006  # Let's use 'birth_year' consistently

# 3. Create the chart
(
  ggplot(ash, aes(x="year", y="Total"))
  + geom_line(color="blue", size=1)
  # Use birth_year here to match your variable above
  + geom_vline(xintercept=birth_year, linetype="dashed", color="red")
  + scale_x_continuous(format="d")
  + labs(
    title="Historical Popularity of the Name 'Ash'", 
    x="Year", 
    y="Births")
)

title: Client Report - What’s in a Name?
text:
QUESTION 2
Think of a unique name from a famous movie. Plot the usage of that name and see how changes line up with the movie release. Does it look like the movie had an effect on usage?
I decided to chose the name Elsa. It’s a very unique name, but after the movie Frozen came out I was curious how it had affected people. And as you can clearly see the spike in it’s popularity is astonding because the movie was released november 2013, and because of that I made the line in the year 2014, because it was basically released then.


Show the code
# Q2
# frozen was released november 2013 so lets look 2014
elsa = df.filter(pl.col("name") == "Elsa")
release = 2014


(
  ggplot(elsa, aes(x="year", y="Total"))
  +geom_line(color="purple", size=1)
  +geom_vline(xintercept=release, linetype="dashed", color="green")
  +scale_x_continuous(format="d")
  +labs(
    title="Trend of the name Elsa, Frozen came out 2014",
    x="Year",
    y="Births"
  )
)

title: Client Report - What’s in a Name?
text:
QUESTION 3
Mary, Martha, Peter, and Paul are all Christian names. From 1920 - 2000, compare the name usage of each of the four names in a single chart. What trends do you notice?
A trend that I’ve noticed with their names is that Mary greatly outweighed the other names up until 1970. This makes sense considering Mary is the name of Jesus’s mother, thus giving it a more holy meaning. Whereas Paul although not as popluar as Mary continuouls stay’s in second throughout the timeline given. And as we reach the 1970 and further the names then start to even out in popularity


Show the code
# Q3

names_to_find = ["Mary", "Martha", "Peter", "Paul"]

names= df.filter(
  (pl.col("name").is_in(names_to_find)) &
  (pl.col("year") >= 1920) &
  (pl.col("year") <= 2000)
)


(
  ggplot(names, aes(x="year", y="Total", color = "name")) 
  +geom_line(size = 1)
  +scale_x_continuous(format="d")
  +labs(
    title = "Christian names from 1920 - 2000",
    x = "Year",
    y= "Number",
    color = "Name"
  )
  
)
